title: Composting livestock manure properly
subtitle: Turning a muck heap into safe usable compost over twelve months.
excerpt: Fresh livestock manure burns soil, carries weed seeds, and contains parasite eggs from worming residues. Composting it correctly for twelve months gives a stable nutrient-rich soil amendment safe for the vegetable patch. A walk through stacking, turning, the temperature curve, and how to tell when it is ready.
sourceNotes: DEFRA storage of farmyard manure code. Cornell Small Farms manure composting notes. AHDB nutrient management guide. John Seymour, The New Complete Book of Self-Sufficiency, muck heap chapter.

glossaryTerms.definition: The traditional name for the stacked pile of livestock bedding and manure outside the barn. The heap heats up and breaks down over months into compost the gardens and pasture can use.

Heat-loving. Used here for the stage of composting when bacteria raise the internal temperature of the heap to between fifty and seventy degrees, killing weed seeds and pathogens. The heap must reach and hold this range for several days for sanitation to work.

recipeTools.notes: A long-probe model that reaches the centre of the heap.

content.text: Fresh livestock manure straight from the barn is too rich and too raw to put on a vegetable bed. It burns soft roots, brings in weed seeds, and may carry parasite eggs from any wormer residues. A
muck heap
composted for twelve months turns the same material into a stable dark crumbly soil amendment that goes on any bed without damage. The trick is in the stacking, the turning, and the patience.
Siting the heap

Pick a spot on level ground away from any watercourse (the welfare code requires ten metres minimum from a stream, river, or ditch). A concrete or compacted earth base is fine; rain leaches nutrients into the surrounding soil if the heap sits on bare ground, so a slight slope away from any path is wise. The heap should be accessible by wheelbarrow from the barn and by tractor or hand fork for turning. Most smallholdings build two bays side by side, so one heap matures while the next year's is filling.
Stacking the heap

A working heap is at least one metre cubed; smaller heaps do not retain heat well enough to compost properly. Stack the heap in layers as you muck out: a layer of bedding-and-dung from the barn, a sprinkle of garden waste or straw if the muck is wet, a scatter of soil or finished compost to introduce bacteria. Keep the sides vertical rather than sloping; a tall narrow heap holds heat better than a wide flat one. Cover the top with a tarp in heavy rain to stop the heap waterlogging, but leave it open in normal weather to let air in.

Aim for a moisture content of about sixty per cent: damp like a wrung-out sponge. Too wet and the heap turns anaerobic and smells of ammonia. Too dry and the bacteria stop working. A handful squeezed should hold together without dripping water.
The temperature curve

A well-built heap heats up in the first week as bacteria break down sugars and proteins in the bedding. The centre of the heap should reach fifty to sixty degrees within ten days and hold there for two to three weeks. This is the
thermophilic

stage; it kills weed seeds, parasite eggs, and most pathogens. A compost thermometer with a long probe lets you measure the centre weekly. A heap that does not heat is too wet, too dry, too small, or too low in nitrogen; check the moisture and add fresh muck or grass clippings to lift the nitrogen.
After the first hot stage the temperature falls back into the thirties and the heap enters a longer cooler curing stage. Fungi and worms take over from the bacteria; the heap shrinks and darkens.
Turning the heap

Turning aerates the heap and brings the cooler outer material into the hot core for a second round of breakdown. For a small heap a hand fork works; for anything larger the front bucket of a compact tractor is the working tool. Turn at three months, again at six, and one final turn at nine. A heap turned three times and left to cure for a further three months is ready at twelve months for use on the vegetable patch.

A heap left to sit without turning still composts, but unevenly: the centre breaks down well and the outer skin barely composts at all. The unturned heap takes around eighteen months to mature and still leaves a layer of unrotted material on top.
How to know it is ready

Finished compost is dark brown to black, crumbly, and smells of woodland soil rather than of ammonia or barn. The original bedding (straw, wood shavings) is no longer recognisable. The heap has shrunk to about half its starting volume. The temperature is at ambient and stays there even when probed deep. Earthworms run through the centre. If any of those signs are missing, leave it longer; an extra two or three months rarely hurts.
Using the finished compost

Spread finished compost on the vegetable beds in late autumn or early spring, about five centimetres deep. Rake or rotovate it into the top spit. A horse-muck compost runs richer and suits hungry crops such as brassicas, courgettes, and potatoes. A sheep-muck compost is more balanced and works as a general soil feed. Chicken-muck compost is high in nitrogen. Use it sparingly around heavy feeders, or water it down as a liquid feed.
Storage and the welfare code

Farm yard manure storage is covered by the welfare code on watercourses and nutrient management. The key rules: stay ten metres from any watercourse, do not stack within fifty metres of a borehole or spring, and do not stack on a slope that drains into a public road or neighbouring property. Most smallholders are well clear of these limits; the rules apply mainly to commercial holdings.
What you might have found

attrs.heading: Heap problems
attrs.intro: Most heap problems are moisture, nitrogen, or size.

items.symptom: Heap smells of ammonia and feels wet to the touch.
items.cause: Too wet, anaerobic in the centre.
items.fix: Turn the heap fully and add dry straw, sawdust, or shredded cardboard as you turn. Cover with a tarp until the wet weather passes. The smell clears within a few days once air is back.

items.symptom: Heap is dry and not heating up.
items.fix: Water the heap as you turn it. Add fresh muck and grass clippings if available. Build the heap up to at least a metre cubed if it is currently smaller.

items.symptom: Heap heating to seventy degrees and steaming heavily.
items.cause: Very high nitrogen; the heap may go past the safe range and kill the beneficial bacteria.
items.fix: Turn the heap to release heat. Add some carbon-rich material (straw, wood shavings, cardboard) as you turn. Aim to bring the centre back down to fifty to sixty degrees.

items.symptom: Weed seedlings sprouting on the surface of an old heap.
items.cause: Heap did not reach thermophilic temperature on its outer skin.
items.fix: Turn the heap to bring the outer skin into the hot centre. Cover the top with a tarp to suppress further germination. Spread the finished compost on areas where weeds are less of a problem (orchard floor rather than the vegetable patch).

content.text: A working muck heap is one of the simplest pieces of smallholding infrastructure and one of the most valuable. The first year takes a learning curve; from the second year onwards the routine is built in and finished compost feeds the gardens, the orchard, and the pasture for the rest of the year.
